key_points: Heredity is controlled by genes which are segments of DNA present on chromosomes
During reproduction, genes from both parents combine to determine offspring traits
Mendel’s experiments established the laws of inheritance including dominance, segregation, and independent assortment
Homologous chromosomes carry genes controlling the same traits
Variations arise due to recombination of genes during meiosis and random fertilization
Sex determination in humans occurs through X and Y chromosomes
Genetic inheritance plays an important role in evolution and biodiversity
examples: Monohybrid cross between TT and tt results in Tt offspring showing dominant trait
Dihybrid cross between RrYy plants produces phenotypic ratio of 9:3:3:1
Human blood group system shows codominance with IA and IB alleles
Snapdragon flower colour demonstrates incomplete dominance producing pink flowers
Sex determination occurs when X or Y chromosome from sperm fertilizes the ovum
formulas: Alleles → Alternative forms of a gene controlling a trait
exam_tips: Practice detailed Mendelian cross problems including dihybrid crosses
Understand chromosomal basis of inheritance and meiosis
Revise concepts of codominance, incomplete dominance, and multiple alleles
Study sex determination carefully as it is commonly asked in exams
Focus on reasoning questions related to variation and evolution
